fact_texts: Rousseau lived at home in Wootton, Staffordshire, near the Peak Country, paying a rent of £30 a year and receiving a £100 pension from George III, which he declined after the first annual payment.
Rousseau's father was banished after a quarrel with a man named G----, leading Rousseau to be sent to Bossey to learn Latin under uncle Bernard.
In 1770, Rousseau settled in Paris, living continuously for seven years on a street that now bears his name, and earned a living by copying music.

In June 1766, Rousseau wrote a violent letter to Hume calling him 'one of the worst of men.' In spring 1767, he fled first to Spalding, Lincolnshire, then to Calais, landing in May 1767, and thereafter frequently changed residence, earning the title 'Voyageur Perpetuel.'
Rousseau had a brother seven years older who was neglected, became a libertine, disappeared to Germany, and was never heard from again.
Rousseau had a mistress named Madame de Warens, whom he called his 'Mamma'.

The user describes their upbringing: staying with uncle, learning drawing and Euclid, but facing limited instruction due to family neglect, and considering a ministerial career despite financial constraints.

Rousseau was born in Geneva in 1712 to Isaac Rousseau, a watchmaker with a modest income divided among fifteen children, and Susannah Bernard, daughter of Mons. Bernard, a minister; his mother died during his birth and his father never fully recovered from the loss.

Rousseau had no recollection of learning to read before age five or six; he read romances with his mother and father after supper, often all night, developing facility. After summer 1719 he studied history, biographies, and classical works, with Plutarch becoming his favorite author, republican spirit and love of liberty.
Rousseau renounced his father's duty but later expiated it through a long and bitter repentance.
Rousseau's father was banished after a quarrel with a man named G----, leading Rousseau to be sent to Bossey to learn Latin under uncle Bernard.
Lord Byron's writings influenced Rousseau, and Byron expressed sympathy with Rousseau's character in some stanzas of 'Childe Harold'.
Rousseau's works 'Reveries d'un Promeneur Solitaire' and 'La Nouvelle Heloise' are closely allied in tone and spirit to the Confessions.

In June 1766, Rousseau wrote a violent letter to Hume calling him 'one of the worst of men.' In spring 1767, he fled first to Spalding, Lincolnshire, then to Calais, landing in May 1767, and thereafter frequently changed residence, earning the title 'Voyageur Perpetuel.'
In 1770, Rousseau settled in Paris, living continuously for seven years on a street that now bears his name, and earned a living by copying music.

Rousseau's remains were first interred in the Ile des Peupliers, later moved to the Pantheon after the Revolution, and a statue modeled by Pradier was erected on an island in the Rhone by the Government of Geneva.

Rousseau had a profound sense of justice and a real desire for the improvement and advancement of the race.
